speaker: interviewer
text: This is Tape number 2, an interview with Dr. Andreas Chrambach. You were in the process of telling me about your trip to Budapest with-- with passports that were somewhat fishy. Is that what you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't really know whether these were real passports or whether they are falsified.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the general, he was--

speaker: interviewee
text: He persuaded the officials at the border to let us through, in spite of fact that we obviously-- we obviously were not-- not Hungarians. And then the dramatic thing, of course, happened in the next train station, where my father waited at the train station, for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. First station after the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hungary, right. Right. So that's when we were reunified for the first time in more than two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you remember about the first meeting with your dad after-- after that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was obviously exuberant, right? And we-- we felt that we had come out alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- was he actually working in Hungary at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was always working, because obviously he had to feed his family. But he was working with great difficulty, doing all sorts of things, crazy things, in part that I remember. At that time-- I think at that time, he was still working in some ways for the Hohenlohes, because they had some property in Hungary and in Romania, and I think in connection with that. But that also petered out after some time. And I remember one job he did was to provide the Hungarian army with-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with a tire repair mode. He located some people in Hamburg, a man by the name of [? Thiesel ?], who introduced him into this process, new process about fixing up tires. I think Father spent a couple of weeks with the Honved, the Hungarian army, fixing their tires, I think with a pretty negative conclusion. They concluded this was not working. But he tried everything crazy like this to keep us alive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Obviously, we lived on arrival in Budapest the four of us, in one room, in a rented pension. We rented one room in a pension. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had no cooking facilities, so we had food delivered from a neighborhood restaurant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember where that was. [HUNGARIAN]. And the owner-- the owner of that-- the reason why-- this was actually a very interesting pension, because the-- the owner was a Berliner lady, who had been the secretary of my Hungarian uncle, whom I haven't talked about yet, while he was working in the German war ministry during the First World War as an-- as an Austrian [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [GERMAN], as an officer making contact between the Austrian and German armies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Information officer?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's not information. An officer whose job was to communicate between the two armies. So anyway, this lady was his secretary. And I suspect at one point during the First World War, they must have lovers, because she never failed to mention how good-looking an officer my uncle was during that time. Anyway, she, in the later course became secretary as well as lover with a Jewish physician, by the name of Anheim in Berlin, who-- who I remember quite well, who was a-- was a physician working in working-class areas in Berlin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so I think he saved them by bringing them to Hungary illegally. And he lived illegally in the same apartment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I will never quite forget that he-- you know, he couldn't leave except at night, for fear of being captured. He grew mice for scientific experiments to keep himself amused, which of course we boys thought was a big thing. He had an enormous library there, obviously an intellectual.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anheim was his name. And I remember that one night he didn't return from going in the street. They picked him up, and he was put to a Hungarian labor camp and finally ended up in some concentration camp in-- in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your uncle.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, yeah, my uncle is also an interesting-- my uncle was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was a mine engineer in Spain, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] prior to First World One-- First World One, then during the First World War he became an [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Austro-Hungarian officer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, before he became that, he made-- he decided to marry a very wealthy Hungarian aristocrat. And the-- the price for that marriage was that he had to abandon his profession. They didn't think it was worthy of their class to have somebody a mine engineer in Spain. He made a tremendous mistake of giving this up for his wife and then lived with this terribly spoiled wife, rich wife for the rest of his life doing nothing, except hunting. An absolutely wasted life. He perished in the ghetto of Budapest at the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he live as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. He was the one who was-- was baptized, I think, together with my father. I mean, he was-- but he was not-- I don't think he was as religious as my parents were. They were probably the sort of Catholics that go to mass on Christmas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why he ended up in a ghetto, if he wasn't acting as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I mean, he was obvious-- this was after fascism had come to Hungary in 1944 with the same-- same race laws that Germany had, so there was no question about it. He, in fact, did-- did wear the yellow star and-- and had to move out of his apartment in Buda. And both he and his wife ended up in the Budapest ghetto. And I think he collapsed on the street at one point there. But these were-- these were the really-- at the time in Hungary, it was when the government was run by rabid fascists. I mean, even more rabid probably than much of what had happened in Germany. So anyway, so that was Uncle Fritz. He was also, as I say, I mean, he was very rich from his wife, but also extremely stingy. So he never helped us, I don't think. And I remember my father complaining always when they took the tramway together that he had to pay the tram fare for my rich uncle because he never had-- you know, all of his money was in stocks. He would never part with anything. So this was a rather curious relationship. And also, you know, just to show you what sort of bastard he really was, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was another brother who ended up in America-- we may have to talk to him later-- about him later-- who-- who immigrated to America in 1936, saying that he was a bachelor, because he couldn't otherwise get a visa. He left his wife and his children in Breslau to perish. And Father saved both of these kids. One of them he sent to Sweden. Some Lutheran organization took him in and he ended up as a stable boy in Northern Sweden. The other one, the girl, ended up in England with Quakers, also a drama story by herself. She's still alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, so when the-- the boy arrived in Budapest when we were living in this one room. And my father went to his brother, begging him to do something for this kid. He could have stayed with us, had we had-- you know, Father couldn't feed our family. But he never budged a finger. So Father found this Lutheran organization that flew him to Sweden. And I think his first years in Sweden must have been so terrible. He's never-- he has become a very strange guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, but it saved his life. His mother was deported to Lodz. We still got a postcard in Hungary from the Lodz ghetto. And that was the last thing we heard of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your father's wealth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, nothing, of course. It was all left in Germany. We came to Hungary with absolutely nothing. And Father also left Germany without valid papers, without a penny, so the wealth all stayed behind and everything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- you did not speak Hungarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the beginning. In fact, we didn't even learn it in the beginning, because my father had decided that he wanted us to go to Jesuit school in Belgium. And since-- well, I was 11 at that time. We didn't speak French, so we made a colossal, eight-month effort prior to the opening of the school year, September 29 in Belgium, to learn French. In fact, we did it eight hours a day. They hired a private tutor to us. And for the rest of my life, I will be a little bit French from that time. And it has obviously stayed with me. But Belgium never materialized, because in September '39, Belgium was occupied.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it is then that-- that Father tried and succeeded to get us into the German school. Now, this was the official Nazi school in Budapest. And the only reason I think why they took us, I think, was first of all, that the director was very-- a very anti-fascist sort of guy. And many of the teachers were in Hungary for that reason, although some of them had Nazi party posts. But that was just to cover up their own safety. But it was-- they were very magnanimous in taking us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at one point or another, we also had German passports, Fascist passports, which again has to do with this Aryanization process, because at one point or another, I think the German embassy in Budapest took the attitude that as long as this process was not decided, they would-- they would give us German passports. That also may have helped to keep us in that school. But of course, it was-- OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as soon as we were in that school-- this is the same-- same sort of school that my children now visit in Washington. Instead of having English taught in English, they had Hungarian and Hungarian history and Hungarian geography taught in Hungarian. And so when we entered the school, I remember memorizing pages and pages of text in Hungarian that we didn't really know, we didn't understand. But we could spit it back and do our homework that way. It was very hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then of course it was time we learned Hungarian, as kids do. And in fact, at the end, in 1944, I took my baccalaureate, both in Hungarian and in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's comparable to your high school graduation?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the high school graduation, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about school in-- in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, school in Budapest was-- was-- was, again, a time of-- of really great peace. And we were not harassed. We were not harassed because the teachers, as I say, didn't harass us. We were a little bit in a funny position, because all the Hungarian kids belonged to Hungarian youth organization . All the German kids belonged to the Hitler Jugend. And we didn't belong to anything. But you see, since, I think, the teachers didn't make a big fuss about this, nobody else much did. And we were not harassed in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a big school, a small school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, it was a big school. It was probably, oh, maybe 500, 600 kids, exactly of the same type that my kids go to now. It was a good school and rather rigorous. A lot of Hungarians sent their kids there, because it was for them a way to give their children a more cosmopolitan education. They were very serious about teaching Western culture, and of course, particularly German literature and history and culture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did there come a time when your normal life, so to speak, became disrupted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: March, 1944, it was all over. In March, 1944, suddenly the streets of Budapest were filled with German soldiers and SS. And a rabidly fascist government came to power. Well, stepwise. First a less rabid one, under Imredy, and then a really bad one, very soon after.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's when peace ended. And in fact, we had to have our high school-- the-- the-- the Hungarian children of Jewish background and us were given a I think two- or three-week early high school graduation exam, just because the school didn't feel they could hold us any longer, so we made a baccalaureate examination that was a few weeks ahead of the rest of the class. What was your status at the time, as a Jew or as a German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean, as far as the Hungarians concerned, of course we were-- we were both of these, both Jews and Germans. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So, you know, very soon I think the yellow star policy-- I mean, the legislation arrived in Hungary. And as I mentioned before, we never-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we never obeyed that law. We never wore yellow stars, because we felt [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so strongly that this was not our identity. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So-- but all of my classmates with whom I made the baccalaureate wore the yellow star and were ghettoized, which of course, we were not either, until our arrest, which came [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for my father, a few days after the 20th of July putsch, and for us, maybe two weeks later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any Jewish friends that you went to school with that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, yes. I had-- well, maybe not friends. But I had certainly at least a couple of Jewish classmates. I'm not sure really whether these were religious Jews either. But that I don't know. They were certainly ethnic Jews. No, maybe not even that. They were probably ethnic Hungarians. It's very hard to-- to say that. All I know is that under that legislation, everybody was defined by the religion of their grandparents. They were Jews just like us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never lived in the ghetto itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I was-- we lived a perfectly [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] independent life until the days of our arrest in Budapest. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So describe the arrest. How did that come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] maybe I should start out with the arrest of my father, because that preceded everything else. And I think our arrest came under the general heading of Sippenhaft, the responsibility of the family. That was one of the fascist views, that whenever anybody was punished for one reason or another, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the family also had a responsibility. And if the father was shot, usually wife and children and babies, everybody was shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, well, my father's story [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] actually goes back a few years. And I think it's-- I can't explain his arrest without explaining what preceded it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In 1943, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Father received a note from the [GERMAN], the famous Aryanization authority in Berlin, that they wanted to see him. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] German patriot as he was, and also, I think, accustomed to follow authority, took the next train to Berlin, where he was instantly arrested.

speaker: interviewee
text: He ended up, for reasons that I don't know, in a prison in Vienna [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and was apparently during that time [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] threatened with-- sort of daily-- with being taken from the prison to Mathausen, which was the prime death factory among the Austrian concentration camps. It was actually a-- a quarry, like Buchenwald. Well, anyway, so he was in prison. And then the next thing we knew-- well, we knew he was in prison really from our contact in Vienna, which was, again, General [INAUDIBLE]. So then one day, maybe eight weeks after he disappeared, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Father was back. And what apparently had happened is that his right-wing, monarchist friends in the army had managed to get him out of prison and assigned a German soldier to him, to walk through the woods from Slovakia into Hungary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then at the next train station he took the train, and there he was. Well, my conjecture-- well, very soon after that time, suitcases of British pounds-- he used to white British pound notes, and I don't know whether that was 20 pounds or 100 pounds or what-- arrived in our-- in our apartment in Buda. And it was-- my conjecture is obviously that the price for freeing him was that from now on, you work for us. us being, I think the anti-Spanish group of the army, which in Berlin was just headed by [INAUDIBLE] Canaris and probably [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had a great high concentration of royalist-minded conservative officers of my father's type, of which he probably knew some from his days in the army. But that's conjecture, again. Anyway, then he-- OK. What happened with these pounds is that Father converted them to gold, I think on the black market or whatever, banks, I have no idea. But the next thing I knew-- first of all, that after years of poverty, we were suddenly living in wealth again. There was a lot of money around. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I remember Father very proudly one day bringing home two wooden boxes, chocolate boxes by a manufacturer that still exists, called Gerbeaud, in Budapest-- in fact, we have a Gerbeaud box here somewhere, they are [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 30 by 20 centimeter wooden boxes filled with gold coins that he had-- had acquired as a kickback for the family, which of course made him a very rich man. Well, OK. He kept these in a hotel safe in Pest. This was the Hotel Vadaszkurt, or translated, the-- the hunter's horn, the Hunter's Horn Hotel, where he had a permanent room. And that also-- you know, we have to-- all conjecture from a child's viewpoint who really has never been talked to about these things, so [INAUDIBLE]. But I would-- certainly, Father maintained that room for years. And I think what had happened in the Aryanization process is that they-- they required him to divorce my wife. I think they were divorced at that time. So officially, he had to have another residence. And that was it. Well, after his-- I think were his protectors until that day vanished after July 20th, 1944, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some SS men apparently came to the hotel, forced him to-- to hand over the gold, and arrested him. And then-- OK. So two weeks later or so, I was in Pest for something, I don't know. Came home, nobody was at home. I mean my mother, my brother were not. It turns out they had been picked up by SS men in the morning. I sat around the apartment a little bit, stupid kid that I was, totally unable to understand at the time, and really just waited until they picked me up. And we ended up, all four of us, in the main Gestapo prison in Hungary, that is located at Fo Utca, of which I also have taken numerous photographs if you want to see it. It still exists and has become very famous, because at that time already it housed the Hungarian government of that time. And later it was a place where Nagy was executed by the Communists. It also became later the main Communist detention center for Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The street Fo Utca, F-O-- O umlaut, yeah, F-O umlaut, Utca street. Like [HUNGARIAN.]

speaker: interviewer
text: By the way, did you still live in that one-room apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. No. No. You had

speaker: interviewer
text: Moved?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Since wealth-- yeah, of course not. Yeah, no. Since-- since-- since I think since the gold days we bought a marvelous apartment in the elegant part of the city, in Buda, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on what was then called [HUNGARIAN] 55. Now it's called [HUNGARIAN], very nice area. And had it beautifully furnished, and things changed radically I think pretty much since 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know why your father was put in prison? Did he tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course not. I've never talked to him again, except in a-- in a cattle wagon taking us from prison to Vienna. But we didn't talk about-- we-- we didn't talk about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, you know, from my understanding today, the main reason why they got him was not so much his involvement with the putsch people, which was quite secondary. I mean, it was not even secondary. He was fifth-- fifth grade. He was a little cogwheel in a machine. Otherwise, they would have executed us like they executed hundreds-- hundreds of people involved in that plot. They hanged them in Plotzensee. So the mere reason-- the mere fact that Father wasn't hanged tells me that they didn't assign too much importance to his economic role there. I think that simply the-- the SS people got-- got an idea that somebody had a lot of gold and wanted to steal it into their pockets. I'm sure that's where it ended up. It was the gold they arrested him for, not his involvement, because his-- his economic involvement in selling these falsified pounds, you know, obviously not only served the-- the organization of the German putsch, it also served the-- served the war effort, because it undermined Britain's economy. And undoubtedly much of that gold went for purposes that Father would not have-- could not have subscribed to. So-- so there was sort of an army service.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't think-- the mere fact that we were not-- that Father was not-- not shot and that and we were-- we were not I think tells me that the main-- the main reason for that arrest was simply the gold. This is the end of Tape number 2.
